critic_raw: The design retains both models on training+validation combined before final test evaluation. For fraud detection, the validation window (months 13-15) contains fraud patterns from a specific period. Retraining on combined training+validation data means the model has seen the complete fraud pattern landscape up to month 15 before predicting on months 16-18. If a new fraud wave emerges in months 16-18 that wasn't represented in months 1-15, both models would fail similarly. But the design's claim that 'the split mirrors production deployment' is only partially true — in production, models would typically be retrained more frequently on more recent data, not on a static 15-month window.
defender_raw: The design uses a 15-month training window (months 1-15 combined) for the final model — this is a reasonable approximation of a production training window. The claim that 'the split mirrors production deployment' refers to the temporal ordering (trained on past, evaluated on future), not the exact retraining frequency. Novel fraud waves in months 16-18 would affect both models equally, which is an honest reflection of the deployment challenge. The temporal stratification secondary metric (performance by month within test period) explicitly detects if model performance degrades within the test window, indicating concept drift. This is a transparent acknowledgment of the deployment limitation.
adjudication_raw: One concern: static 15-month training window not fully representative of production retraining frequency. Addressed: temporal ordering is the key deployment mimic; monthly stratification detects within-test drift; novel fraud waves affect both models equally. Verdict: defense_wins.
